lines.text: Chapter two, section ten. Test yourself.
This section applies the concepts from the chapter to concrete no-limit hold'em scenarios, all played at a nine-handed table with two and five dollar blinds and five-hundred-dollar effective stacks.
Working through these examples step by step reveals how polarized and condensed range theory translates directly into in-game decisions.
First scenario: the first player to act pre-flop raises to twenty dollars and you call from the big blind with the seven of hearts and seven of spades.
The flop comes queen of diamonds, seven of clubs, three of hearts.
You have flopped bottom set — a very strong hand.
Do you bet or check?
The correct answer is check.
Even though you have an extremely strong hand, your overall range as a big blind caller is condensed.
By calling a pre-flop raise from an early position raiser, your range is full of medium-strength hands and does not contain the premium holdings the raiser could reasonably have.
Playing according to your range means playing consistently with all the hands you could hold in this situation, not just the specific hand you happen to have today.
Checking with your set of sevens is the correct play because it is consistent with how you would play all the medium-strength hands in your condensed range.
Betting would be inconsistent and revealing — it would tell your opponent that you have something special, since nothing in the condensed range really benefits from leading out.
Checking allows the pre-flop raiser to construct a polarized betting range, and many of the hands he bets with will be weaker than your set.
You can then decide whether to call or raise after he bets.
If he checks the flop behind, you can revisit the decision on the turn.
Second scenario: now you are the pre-flop raiser, raising in first position with the seven of hearts and seven of spades, and the big blind calls.
The same flop comes: queen of diamonds, seven of clubs, three of hearts.
Your opponent checks.
Do you bet or check?
The correct answer is bet.
As the pre-flop aggressor, you are expected to have the more polarized range.
Your opponent's check is consistent with the condensed range she is playing from the big blind.

Betting your set is the most deceptive thing you can do here, because it is entirely consistent with how you would play many other hands from your polarized range: a continuation bet with two overcards, with an overpair like nines or tens, with air as a bluff.
Because all of those hands look the same when you bet, your strong hand is effectively camouflaged.
Your opponent still has incentive to call even knowing that you could easily have the best hand, because she must also worry about not getting pushed off her equity when you are bluffing.
Third scenario — the conceptual question: you held the same hand on the same board with the same stacks in both examples, yet in the first case you checked and in the second you bet.
What is the difference?
The difference is range, not hand strength.
As the big blind caller, you represent a condensed range, and checking is how you play the entire condensed range consistently.
As the pre-flop raiser, you represent a polarized range, and betting is how you play the entire polarized range consistently.
Strong hands do not have a fixed correct action — the right action for your strong hand is whatever is consistent with the range you are representing in that particular situation.
Fourth scenario: you call from the big blind with the eight of hearts and eight of spades against the same pre-flop raise to twenty dollars.
The flop comes queen of diamonds, seven of clubs, three of hearts.
You check, your opponent bets twenty-five dollars.
Do you fold, call, or raise?
The correct answer is call.
You have already made a good start by checking.
Now you have a medium-strength hand: a pair of eights that beats bluffs but likely loses to many of your opponent's value hands.

Raising is inappropriate here for the same reasons Opal should not raise in the Clairvoyance Game — your hand is not strong enough to raise for value since better hands will not fold, and it is far too strong to fold.
It is a classic bluff-catcher, and bluff-catchers call.
Raising with a pair of eights would be like betting the King in the Clairvoyance Game: your opponent's strong hands call and their weak hands fold, and you accomplish nothing good.
A common concern when calling in this spot is that you might face additional bets on the turn and river that you cannot call.
This concern is valid but should not push you to fold here.
First, your opponent does not know you have eights — for all he knows you could have seven-seven, king-queen, or some other hand that would happily play for a large pot.
That uncertainty constrains his ability to barrel you off your hand.
Second, facing additional pressure is the worst case, not the guaranteed case.
Sometimes he will slow down, and sometimes you will get to showdown and win.

Fifth scenario: same setup — you called from the big blind with eight-eight and check-called the flop, then check-called again when the turn brought the two of clubs and your opponent bet fifty dollars.
The river is the eight of diamonds, giving you a set.
Do you bet or check?
Check, even though you just made a very strong hand.
Your entire line through this hand has told the story of a condensed range: check-call, check-call.
Your opponent has the polarized range and should continue driving the action on the river.
By checking again, you let him do exactly that.
He might bet a hand that loses to your set, giving you a call and a win.
He might bet a bluff, again giving you a profitable call.
If you lead out, you are suddenly acting outside the narrative of your range, which signals strength and causes him to fold the very hands you want to extract value from.

Sixth scenario: you called from the big blind with the eight of diamonds and seven of diamonds, checked-called on a flop of jack of hearts, seven of clubs, three of hearts, and now the turn is the seven of spades.
You have turned trips.
Do you bet or check?
Checking is acceptable, but this is one of those rare cases where betting is worth serious consideration.
Because you are the big blind caller and the board has paired a low card, you are now more likely to have trips than your opponent.
His strong hands — overpairs — will often fear that you hold the seven and may check behind if you give them the chance.
If that happens, you might only win one more bet across the rest of the hand instead of two.
By betting, you present your opponent with a different risk: the risk of folding the best hand to a bluff.
You can balance your trip sevens with bluffs like draws that you check-called the flop with, making your bet range credible.
Seventh scenario: same situation but now you hold the ten of clubs and nine of clubs, giving you an open-ended straight draw on the turn when the seven of spades falls.
Should you bet or check?
Again, betting deserves consideration.
You hold a draw with roughly twenty-three percent equity — a meaningful chance to improve — but you are currently behind anything that would call a bet.
Betting as a bluff here is reasonable, both because you are more likely to have turned trips than your opponent and because your draw gives you outs if called.
Winning the pot immediately is worth more to you than a roughly one-in-four chance of improving to the straight on the river.
Eighth scenario: after a pre-flop raise that you called from the big blind with queen of spades and jack of spades, the flop comes queen of diamonds, seven of clubs, three of hearts.
You check, and your opponent checks behind.
The turn is the two of clubs.
Do you bet or check?
Bet.
When the pre-flop aggressor declines to bet the flop, that is a signal that his range has weakened.
He likely holds either a weak hand he declined to bluff or a medium-strength hand that prefers not to grow the pot.
This is your cue to take the lead with a polarized range.
Queen-jack is a strong hand on this board — not quite the nuts, but strong enough to bet for value.
Your opponent's range, having checked back, should contain many hands that will call you with worse: small pairs, ace-high hands, various draws.
Ninth scenario: same pre-flop and flop setup but now you hold ace of hearts and six of hearts, and you are on the turn after your opponent checked back the flop.
Do you bet or check?
Check.
While your opponent's flop check does give you an opportunity to bluff, you should not bluff with every weak hand in your range.
The correct approach is to bet a polarized range that includes your strong hands and a fraction of your weaker hands as bluffs.
Ace-six with a low flush draw has at most three outs to improve against anything that would call, making it a poor bluffing candidate.
Even if your opponent folds, he is probably folding a hand you already beat — not exactly a triumph.
Better bluffing candidates here are hands with more substantial draws, like five-four of diamonds or eight-six of clubs, which have much better chances of improving to winning hands if called.
You get to bluff some of the time after your opponent checks back, but not too much, and not with your worst bluffing candidates.
